title: `machine start` signal handler orphans VM by calling os.Exit(1) without cleanup
body:
### Issue Description

The signal handler in `pkg/machine/shim/host.go` (added in #25832, extended in #26950) catches SIGINT/SIGTERM/SIGPIPE during `machine start` and correctly resets `Starting: false` in the JSON config. However, it then calls `os.Exit(1)`, which skips all Go defers, including the cleanup callbacks that stop gvproxy and the VM.

If the signal arrives after `StartVM()` has already launched the VM process, the result is:

- Machine shows as "Currently running" but has no API forwarding
- `podman ps` → "connection refused"
- `podman machine stop` hangs

There's also a related issue on the Podman Desktop side https://github.com/podman-desktop/podman-desktop/issues/9670 which we could resolve by sending correctly the SIGTERM, but we also need this fix to avoid getting a machine that is not responding.

### Steps to reproduce the issue

WARNING: this will start a non responding machine, and you may have to reset it.

Steps to reproduce the issue
1.  Start with a stopped machine. Use `podman machine ls` to check the status. 
2. Send a SIGTERM before the machine had time to start:
In zsh with nodejs on Mac for instance:
`node -e "const c = require('child_process').spawn('podman', ['machine', 'start']); setTimeout(() => { c.kill('SIGTERM'); setTimeout(() => process.exit(), 2000) }, 1500)"`
3. Check that the machine is running:  `podman machine ls`: "Currently running"
3. Try to list containers, or stop the machine `podman ps` results in an error: "Cannot connect to Podman." and "podman machine stop" hangs.

### Describe the results you received

When sending SIGTERM during `podman machine start`, I get a running machine without an API. I cannot list containers, nor can I stop the machine.

### Describe the results you expected

When sending SIGTERM during `podman machine start`, I would like the machine to be stopped.

### podman info output

comments.body:
Thank you for the detailed bug report and analysis @simonrey1 

Here are a few notes after debugging the problem on macOS and Windows.

### Problem 1: The `machine start` rollback problem

After `VMProvider.StartVM()`, if there is an error or a termination signal, we don't stop the just-started VM. The cleanup gortoutines are executed, but we never try to stop the VM. If we are lucky (in the case of `CTRL+C`, for example), the stop signal is also propagated to the process that starts the VM. But this isn't always the case, and in the Podman Desktop shutdown example, the VM startup process continues without interruption.

### Problem 2: Duplicated Signal handling
 
There are two distinct goroutines that handle interruption signals when the podman machien is started:

https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/a36dea00052c1b474d9de0b703418d15967fc185/pkg/machine/shim/host.go#L527-L543

https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/a36dea00052c1b474d9de0b703418d15967fc185/pkg/machine/shim/host.go#L577

And after #26950, the two goroutines are inconsistent: the second one doesn't handle `SIGPIPE`.

### Note 1: `CTRL+C` works, `subprocess.kill('SIGTERM')` doesn't

On macOS there is a subtle but important difference when Podman is interrupted with `CTRL+C` from the terminal or from Podman Desktop NodeJS process:

- With `CTRL+C` the terminal sends the signal to `podman` and its child processes (`krunkit` and `gvproxy` in `libkrun` case)
- NodeJS `subprocess.kill('SIGTERM')` sends the signal to the `podman` process only

That explains why both @jakecorrenti and @Honny1 commented, in #25832, that the command terminated cleanly.

### Note 2: A possible workaround on macOS

If podman is started from a shell process (with `sh -c`, for example), the signal is also sent to the child processes.

With a slightly modified `subprocess.spawn` command, podman machine results as stopped after the SIGTERM is sent to the `podman machine start` command:

```bash
node -e "const c = require('child_process').spawn('sh', ['-c', 'podman', 'machine', 'start']); setTimeout(() => { c.kill('SIGTERM'); setTimeout(() => process.exit(), 2000) }, 1500)"
```

### Note 3: Windows peculiarities

As on macOS, `CTRL+C` in a PowerShell terminal is handled correctly: the signal is propagated to `podman machine start` child processes as well.

NodeJS 's `subprocess.kill('SIGTERM')` on Windows behaves differently compared to Linux and macOS: the process is killed abruptly, as with a `SIGKILL` signal, and the program cannot handle it. The behavior is [documented](https://nodejs.org/api/child_process.html#subprocesskillsignal). And using `powershell -Command` to run `podman machine start` doesn't help either.

We have tested using `taskkill` to programmatically send a termination signal that propagates to the child processes as well (`taskkill /T /PID <podman machine start PID>`), but it fails with the message: "This process can only be terminated forcefully (with /F option)".

Hello, 
Thanks a lot for your investigation.

> ### Note 2: A possible workaround on macOS
> If podman is started from a shell process (with `sh -c`, for example), the signal is also sent to the child processes.

I tried this solution on my fork and it seems to be working in my manual tests.

@jiridostal Could you also want to try to this PR and compare?

https://github.com/simonrey1/podman-desktop/pull/29/changes
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
I think this issue is still valid, we have just provided a workaround (@simonrey1 correct me if I'm wrong)
@jiridostal, I was working on something else, but I am going to get back to this soon. We need to fix that.
